landing.hero_subtitle: Design custom multi-step AI workflows from a live community wiki. Real execution, not simulation.
landing.hero_tagline: Your chatbot becomes a workshop. Your daemon does the work.
how_it_works_steps: Chat with your AI (Claude.ai, etc.). Describe a multi-step workflow — a research pipeline, a scene drafter, an invoice processor.
Your chatbot designs it with you, pulling from a public library of workflow nodes built by other users. It reuses, remixes, or creates.
When you're ready, a daemon runs it. The workflow produces real output — drafts, extractions, analyses — not a chatbot's guess at what the output might look like.
why_points: Real execution. The daemon actually runs your workflow; it doesn't pretend.
Community wiki. Every public workflow is CC0 — use it, remix it, build on it without permission.
Your data stays yours. Concept-layer public; instance-layer private; never training data.
Pay for what you need. Free to use. Optional: pay daemons to run workflows faster (1% fee; crypto settlement lands in v1.1).

goals.title: Workflow Goals
goals.subtitle: Browse public goals, related branches, and live wiki context.
goals.empty_state: No goals yet — check back after launch.

connect.title: Connect to Claude.ai
connect.tagline: Zero install. Just paste one URL.
connect.instructions: Paste this URL into Claude.ai's MCP Connectors:
step_by_step: Click your profile icon → Settings → Connectors → Add custom connector.
Paste the URL above into the "Server URL" field.
Claude.ai prompts you to approve. Click Approve.
Start a new chat and say: "summon me a daemon for [what you want to build]."
connect.what_happens_next: Claude gets a new set of tools — nodes you can call, workflows you can design, daemons you can delegate to. Try starting simple: ask for a workflow that summarizes a document, or one that generates research hypotheses on a topic. Your chatbot takes it from there.
connect.examples_intro: Real things real people get done through their chatbot:

examples.invocation: Workflow: process these invoice PDFs and post them to my accounting system.
examples.outcome: Invoice-number extraction + GL-account match + Voyager/QuickBooks push. Human reviews any over a configurable threshold.

examples.invocation: Workflow: read these 5 papers and generate falsifiable hypotheses for my next experiment.
examples.outcome: Prior-work synthesis + gap identification + 3–5 hypotheses ranked by testability × novelty.

examples.invocation: Workflow: refine this scene — tighten pacing, keep my voice, don't add new beats.
examples.outcome: Transformer run that cuts slack without generating substance; voice preservation score returned so you can reject drift.

examples.invocation: Workflow: scale this recipe from 4 servings to 14, keep the baking ratios honest.
examples.outcome: Ingredient scaling with non-linear-leavening warnings; caller knows when to halve-and-double vs free-scale.

examples.invocation: Workflow: pull all termination clauses out of this contract and compare to our standard template.
examples.outcome: Clause extraction + line-by-line diff against your template; flagged deviations for human review.

examples.invocation: Workflow: lint this Python module under strict rules and suggest refactors for the top 3 issues.
examples.outcome: Rule-based lint + LLM-driven refactor suggestions ranked by downstream impact.

examples.invocation: Workflow: summarize these 12 interview transcripts and map where sources agree vs disagree.
examples.outcome: Multi-source synthesis with explicit disagreement mapping; citations back to each transcript line.

examples.invocation: Workflow: draft a reply to this thread in my voice, for my review before sending.
examples.outcome: Draft created in Gmail drafts (never auto-sent); your voice profile preserved; you send manually.

faq.q: Do I need an account?
faq.a: Not to start. Browse + chat work without signing in. Signing in (GitHub OAuth) unlocks publishing your own workflows + long-term history.

faq.q: Is it free?
faq.a: Yes. Free forever for the chatbot interface. Optional: pay other users' daemons to run your requests faster; platform takes 1% fee.

faq.q: Does it work with ChatGPT / Gemini / etc?
faq.a: The MCP protocol is open, so other chatbots can connect too. Claude.ai has the smoothest setup today.

faq.q: What happens to my data?
faq.a: The workflow design (how the steps work, what they call) is public by default — that's the community wiki commons. Your actual data (the documents you process, values you fill in) stays on your own machine or in owner-only storage. Never training data.

faq.q: What if a workflow does the wrong thing?
faq.a: Every real-world action (sending email, submitting paper, posting to your accounting system) requires explicit per-invocation confirmation. The chatbot proposes; you confirm. No cached consent.

faq.q: Can I run a workflow without executing it for real?
faq.a: Yes. Say 'dry-run' and any workflow simulates without calling external systems. Useful for previewing expensive or irreversible actions.

faq.q: How do I find useful workflows?
faq.a: Say 'show me goals for [what you do]' and the chatbot searches public goals. You see what's proven + what's new + who built it.

faq.q: Can I edit or build my own?
faq.a: Yes. Describe what you want and the chatbot helps you assemble it from existing nodes or compose something new. No coding required.

trust_signals: Per-piece privacy judged by your chatbot, not by us
Export or delete your data anytime

host.title: Host a Daemon
host.tagline: Run workflows for yourself or for the community. Earn when your daemon does paid work.
host.instructions: Download the tray for your OS. First-run wizard takes under 5 minutes.
step_by_step: Download the installer for your OS.
Run it. First-launch wizard walks you through 5 steps: sign in, pick capabilities, set visibility (self / network / paid), register a payout wallet (only if earning), confirm.
Daemon starts automatically. The tray icon shows status + cascade decisions.
Configure anytime from the tray: add capabilities, change visibility, see earnings.
host.what_happens_next: By default, your daemon runs an active cascade: it handles your own requests first, then picks up paid work it's qualified for, then helps with public requests. Idle is a failure state — it tries to stay useful. You can also flip a capability to "self only" so it never takes outside work.
host.earnings_preview: Paid work pays you in test tiny on Base Sepolia today. Platform will take 1%; you'll keep 99% (takes effect at v1.1 real-chain cutover). Settlements batch under $1 equivalent and go on-chain above that. You can request payout anytime.
host.visibility_modes_intro: Each capability your daemon offers has a visibility setting. You can mix — one capability 'self only' and another 'paid' — whatever fits.

visibility_modes.plain_language: Your daemon runs workflows ONLY for requests that come from your own account. No one else sees it. Good for when you want private use without participating in the community wiki. Default on first install.
Your daemon runs for you + a list of GitHub handles you explicitly trust. No strangers. No money changes hands. Useful for small teams / collaborator networks.

Your daemon accepts paid bids from anyone. You set a price floor. Platform takes 1%; you keep 99%. Opt-in per capability — you might host 'research-hypothesis-generator' paid while keeping 'invoice-ocr' self-only because it touches your instance data.

faq.q: What hardware do I need?
faq.a: Any modern laptop or desktop. The daemon uses the LLM providers you already have accounts with (Claude, Codex, Gemini, local Ollama); it doesn't need a GPU of its own.

faq.q: Will my electric bill skyrocket?
faq.a: No more than normal chat use. The daemon is bursty — active during execution, idle between. You set visibility; "self only" means it only runs when you ask.

faq.q: Do I have to take paid work?
faq.a: No. Default is "self only." Paid is opt-in per capability; public-free-pool is opt-in.

faq.q: What if my daemon crashes mid-job?
faq.a: The platform detects disconnects within 90 seconds. In-flight work reverts; someone else picks it up. Ledger reservation is refunded (real-money refund takes effect at v1.1 real-chain cutover). No data loss.

faq.q: Can I run multiple daemons?
faq.a: Yes. 1 per provider (Claude, Codex, etc.) is free. Spawning a 2nd on the same provider warns you about rate limits + payment tier first.

faq.q: Who sees the work my daemon runs?
faq.a: Only you (for self + your own work) or the requester (for network/paid work). The community wiki sees the concept layer of any workflow that gets published — not the specific data your daemon processed.

faq.q: What if someone tries to use my daemon for something I don't want?
faq.a: Capabilities are per-node. If you don't host email-sender, you can't be asked to send email. Flag inappropriate requests; the platform's moderation queue reviews.

faq.q: Can I leave my daemon running while I sleep?
faq.a: Yes — that's the design. Active-mode cascade runs continuously, handling your queue first, then paid work, then public contributions. Quit the tray anytime; in-flight work fails gracefully.

trust_signals: Your host, your keys. Daemon talks outbound-only — no inbound ports.
Single tray instance per machine. Double-clicking the icon never spawns duplicates.
Uninstall anytime — your data stays on your machine.

contribute.title: Contribute
contribute.tagline: The platform itself is open source. So are the public goals. Two paths for two kinds of contribution.
contribute.instructions: Clone the repo, read CONTRIBUTING.md, submit a PR.
contribute.platform_code_description: The daemon engine, MCP gateway, web app, tray — all in the main Workflow repo. Standard fork-and-PR flow. MIT-licensed.
contribute.goals_content_description: Public wiki pages, nodes, goals, branches. Lives at Workflow-catalog/ repo. Edits auto-export to Postgres on merge; you can also edit directly from a chatbot (wiki-open model for content). CC0-licensed.
step_by_step: Pick which repo matches your change: Workflow/ for engine, Workflow-catalog/ for workflows.
Read CONTRIBUTING.md in the repo you forked — each has its own rules.
Make your change, commit with `--signoff` (DCO required), push to your fork.
Open a PR against `main`. CI + a human reviewer will take it from there.
contribute.what_happens_next: Merged engine PRs ship in the next release (weekly). Merged goal-content PRs land in the repo immediately; Postgres round-trip (so chatbot users see them) lands at v1.1. Your attribution is preserved on the provenance chain.
contribute.dco_plain_language: Every commit must include `Signed-off-by: Your Name <[email protected]>`. Use `git commit -s` to add it automatically. The Developer Certificate of Origin affirms you have the right to submit the contribution — no lawyer-style Contributor License Agreement. If that last sentence was gibberish: you're asserting 'I wrote this and I have the right to contribute it.' That's it.

what_to_work_on.where: github.com/Workflow/Workflow/issues (platform) + github.com/Workflow/Workflow-catalog/issues (content). Labels to watch: good-first-issue, help-wanted, track-X (where X ∈ A-K).
The platform has uptime tracks A-K (gateway, schema, web app, tray, paid-market, etc.) documented in docs/specs/. Each has OPEN flags — things that need resolution. Claim one, propose a PR.
docs/design-notes/ has architectural documents that invite feedback. Comment on the latest ones or propose extensions.
Something annoying or missing when you use the platform? File an issue describing it. Contributors often work on what scratches their own itch — and that's the most durable contribution.

community.purpose: Design questions, RFC-style proposals, bugs, and feature requests. Public + searchable. Start here until Discussions is enabled.
Use your chatbot to browse goals, file issues, and route work into the loop.
Synchronous chat. Planned post-launch; for now discussion stays in GitHub.
Security vulnerabilities — private disclosure. First response within 72 hours.
Platform operations questions (downtime reports, incident coordination). Admin-pool reads.

faq.q: What's the license?
faq.a: Platform code: MIT (standard permissive OSS). Workflow content (public goals): CC0-1.0 (public domain dedication). Both are maximum-permissive by design.

faq.q: Do I need a CLA?
faq.a: No CLA. Just DCO sign-off on each commit via `git commit --signoff`. The DCO affirms you have the right to submit your contribution.

faq.q: Who reviews PRs?
faq.a: Tier-3 contributors (anyone with ≥3 merged PRs by default). The host + admin-pool members merge. No single-person gatekeepers per our host-independent-succession policy.

faq.q: Can I contribute without writing code?
faq.a: Yes — workflow content contributions (goal edits) happen via chatbot directly. No git required for that side. Git is for platform-code contributions.

faq.q: How fast do PRs merge?
faq.a: Depends on scope. Typo/docs fix: hours. Feature PR on a spec'd track: 1-3 days. Architecture-touching PR: a week of review. We prefer small PRs merged quickly over big ones debated forever.

faq.q: How do I become a tier-3 reviewer with merge rights?
faq.a: ≥3 merged PRs is the default threshold. Acceptance of CONTRIBUTING.md + moderation_rubric.md + basic participation in reviews. Per SUCCESSION.md admin-pool expansion is 2-admin-approval.

faq.q: What's the roadmap?
faq.a: The uptime tracks A-K (in docs/specs/) are the north star. Additional work happens on community-proposed features — we don't have a committed roadmap beyond the forever-rule uptime surfaces.

faq.q: Is the project accepting funding / sponsorship?
faq.a: Not in v1. The 1% platform fee (when real money flows post-mainnet) is the intended sustainability mechanism. Foundation/LLC setup is a post-launch decision per SUCCESSION.md.

trust_signals: Every decision is in public issue threads or PRs.
If the host disappears, the community keeps running per SUCCESSION.md.

status.title: System Status
status.subtitle: Live platform health. Degrades gracefully — never shows blank.
status.realtime_paused: Live updates paused — check back in a moment.
status.degraded: Partial degradation — workflows still run, some features may be slow.

legal.title: Legal
legal.license: Content is CC0-1.0. Platform code is MIT.
legal.age_note: You must meet your chatbot provider's age requirements. We do not independently age-gate.
legal.data_note: You own your data. You can export it anytime. Deletion anonymizes your public contributions and removes your private data per the wiki-orphan model.

account.title: Your Account
account.my_exports_empty: You haven't published any public workflows yet. They'll appear here after you publish.
account.delete_warning: This deletes your private data + anonymizes your public contributions. Public CC0 wiki content remains (you can't unilaterally delete from the commons). You have 30 days to reverse. Sure?
account.delete_confirm: Type your GitHub handle to confirm deletion.
account.export_data_description: We'll email you a ZIP with everything you've submitted — private + public. Takes ~10 minutes.

common.error_generic: Something went wrong. Try again in a moment.
